title: Technique: Releasing the Fear of Mistakes
description: This article presents practical ways to address perfectionism by embracing imperfection and easing the fear of mistakes.

steps: Technique: Releasing the Fear of Mistakes
Let’s look at a few simple yet highly effective methods to help you move past perfectionism.
The “Anti-Perfectionism” Log
<activitylink id="naslSLnadfl"></activitylink>
Many perfectionists believe they can only truly enjoy something if it’s done perfectly. We invite you to challenge that belief.
For one week, keep a log of everyday, spontaneous tasks—anything from “brushing your teeth,” “making dinner,” and “preparing a work report” to “cleaning the house.”
In the second column, note how effectively you think the task was completed (as a percentage).
<instagram ids="18130674085439149"></instagram>
In the third column, record how much you enjoyed the task.
Examples might look like this:
• Making dinner – 50% (could have chosen a more complex, tastier recipe) – 90% (simple, tasty, and quick).
• Cleaning the house – 70% (covered the basics; didn’t get to the fridge or stove) – 100% (a tidy home always feels good).
• Preparing a work report – 85% (put in a lot of effort) – 20% (constant thoughts about how to improve it drained the enjoyment).
This exercise helps you see that perfection isn’t required to enjoy the process.
<q data-author="Thomas Watson">Perfection is not a destination; it’s a direction.</q>
Accepting the Possibility of Error
<activitylink id="lsvnnDSnsdl"></activitylink>
Nobody likes mistakes, but for perfectionists they can become a major source of fear.
One of the most effective ways to ease this fear is to learn to accept your mistakes.
Do you notice your heart race at the mere thought of getting something wrong? That’s fear and anxiety at work.
To become more open to mistakes, start by writing a brief essay on why mistakes are normal and why striving for absolute perfection is unrealistic.
Here’s how one person working through perfectionism put it:
<dialog  >My fear of making a mistake comes from black-and-white thinking: one mistake and everything collapses. That’s a distortion. A small mistake can’t erase all the good that was done.</dialog>

<dialog  >Mistakes are useful because they’re how we learn. Without mistakes there is no learning. Since no one is immune to them, it’s important to accept them and keep learning from experience.</dialog>
<dialog  >Acknowledging mistakes lets us adjust our behavior, leading to more satisfying outcomes—and that, in turn, makes us happier and improves quality of life.</dialog>
<dialog  >Most people won’t judge us for mistakes or stop loving us because of them—in the end, everyone slips up, and many feel uneasy around “perfect” people.</dialog>
<dialog  >A mistake is not the end of the world.</dialog>
Of course, making such a list won’t instantly transform you or make you love mistakes, but it’s an important step toward changing your attitude toward imperfection.

Write your own short essay on why making mistakes is normal—even helpful. Remind yourself that even if you slip, the world won’t collapse—it’s a natural part of any process, even if it isn’t the most pleasant one.
